sections.title: Test 1 Portion and Blueprint Focus
sections.content:
The first test portion covers only the topics listed in the school blueprint. These are the structure of a Java program, print commands, the data types byte, short, int, float, double, and String, variables, declaration and initialization, arithmetic operators, operands and expressions, binary operator, and the simple assignment operator =.

The blueprint also shows the main question patterns. Students should be ready to label parts of a program, identify parts in the correct order, answer one mark concept questions, match terms, find the value of variables, and write short Java programs.

key_terms.definition: The exact set of topics prescribed for the test.
The pattern showing the types of questions asked in the test.

tables.title: Blueprint Pattern
rows: Recognise class declaration, main method, brackets, statement, semicolon
Look at the program and number parts
Identify parts in order from the given code
Use correct class, main method, print statements, and syntax

examples: Example blueprint task: label the class declaration, main method, statement, and semicolon.
Example blueprint task: write a Java program to display simple lines of output.

sections.title: Structure of a Java Program
sections.content:
A beginner Java program has two main parts: the class declaration and the main method. The class declaration gives the class its name. The main method is the place where program execution begins.

The class name should match the file name. If the class name is Hello, the file should be saved as Hello.java. Curly brackets show code blocks. The outer brackets belong to the class and the inner brackets belong to the main method. Every Java statement ends with a semicolon.

key_terms.definition: The line that introduces and names the class.
The method from which execution begins.
The punctuation mark that ends a Java statement.

examples: If the class name is Student, the file name should be Student.java.
System.out.println("Hello from Java"); is a statement inside the main method.

sections.title: Print Commands
sections.content:
Java uses print commands to display output. System.out.print() prints output on the same line. System.out.println() prints output and then moves to the next line.

Text values are written inside double quotes. Numbers can also be printed. These output statements are usually written inside the main method.

key_terms.definition: Prints output without moving to the next line.
Prints output and then moves to the next line.
The result displayed on the screen.

tables.title: print and println
rows: Output stays on the same line
Output moves to the next line

examples: System.out.print("Hello "); System.out.print("Java"); prints both words on one line.
Two println statements print on two separate lines.

sections.title: Data Types in the Portion
sections.content:
Data types tell Java what kind of value is being stored. In this test portion, students need to know byte, short, int, float, double, and String. Byte, short, and int are used for whole numbers. Float and double are used for decimal numbers. String is used for text.

Different data types use different amounts of memory and can store different ranges of values. Choosing a suitable data type helps store information correctly.

key_terms.definition: The kind of value a variable can store.
Data types used for whole numbers.
The data type used to store text.

sections.title: Variables, Declaration, and Initialization
sections.content:
A variable is a named storage location used to store a value. Declaring a variable means writing its data type and name. Initializing a variable means giving it a value.

For example, in int number = 10;, int is the data type, number is the variable name, and 10 is the value stored. Students should be able to write simple declarations and initializations using the data types in the portion.

key_terms.definition: A named place in memory used to store a value.
Writing the data type and the variable name.
Assigning a value to a variable.
The actual value written directly in the code.

sections.title: Arithmetic Operators
sections.content:
Arithmetic operators are symbols used to perform mathematical operations. The operators in the test portion are +, -, *, /, and %. The modulus operator % gives the remainder after division.

Students should be able to evaluate simple expressions and find the value of variables using these operators.

key_terms.definition: A symbol used for a mathematical operation.
The operator that gives the remainder after division.
The value left after division.

sections.title: Operands, Expressions, Binary Operator, and Assignment Operator
sections.content:
An operand is the value or variable on which an operator works. In 100 + 800, the operands are 100 and 800, and the operator is +. An expression is a piece of code that produces a value when evaluated.

A binary operator needs two operands. The arithmetic operators in this portion are binary operators. The simple assignment operator is =. It assigns the value on the right side to the variable on the left side. For example, in x = 25, the value 25 is assigned to x.

key_terms.definition: A value or variable on which an operator works.
Code that produces a value when evaluated.
An operator that needs two operands.
The symbol = used to assign a value.

examples: System.out.println(100 + 800); is a statement using an expression.

sections.title: Student Notebook Notes - Program Structure
sections.content:
public class Main // Class declaration - part 1

public static void main(String[] args)
// Main method - part 2

// code to display a simple
message
System.out.println("Hello World");

public is an access modifier, it defines the level of access.

class is the keyword used to define a class.

Class declaration - the name you give your class should start with capital. The file name of your java program should match with the class name. For example, here we have given the class the name "Main" (starting with a capital letter), so the file name will be "Main.java".

Static - can be used without creating object.

void => returns nothing.

Main => is the main method of java class where execution of a code / a program.

String[] args => input list from the user.

public static void main(String[] args) as the main method.

In Java every line of code has to have a semi-colon after it.

System.out.println is used to show information on the screen and move to next line.

sections.title: Student Notebook Notes - Program Writing Practice
sections.content:
1. Write a program to:
Create a class named "Bank".
Declare and initialize variables to store: number of customers, customer name, and account balance.
Display customer name and balance.

2. Write a program to:
Create a class named "Library".
Declare and initialize variables to store: number of books, book name, and book price.
Print book name and price.

3. Write a program to declare and initialize two integers and print their sum.

4. Write a program to declare and initialize two integers and print their difference.

5. Write a program to declare and initialize two integers and print their quotient.

6. Write a program to declare and initialize 2 integers and print their remainder.
examples: Declare and initialize two integers and print their sum, difference, quotient, and remainder.

sections.title: Syntax Rules Cheat Sheet
sections.content:
Java is very strict about punctuation. One missing semicolon or one wrong bracket and your program will not run. Here are the rules to remember.

THE SEMICOLON RULE: Put a semicolon at the end of every statement. A statement is any instruction that tells the computer to do something. Think of it like a full stop at the end of a sentence.

DO NOT put a semicolon after a class declaration or after a method declaration. These use curly brackets instead.

THE CURLY BRACKETS RULE: Curly brackets { } create blocks of code. They always come in pairs. The class body goes inside one pair. The main method body goes inside another pair. Think of them like a box that holds code inside it.

THE ROUND BRACKETS RULE: Round brackets ( ) are used in two places. First, after a method name to hold its parameters. Second, inside print commands to hold what you want to print. Think of them like hands holding something.

THE SQUARE BRACKETS RULE: Square brackets [ ] are used for arrays. You will see them in String[] args in the main method. For now, just remember they go after String in the main method line.

THE DOUBLE QUOTES RULE: Any text you want to print must go inside double quotes. Numbers do not need quotes. If you forget the quotes around text, Java will think the text is a variable name and give an error.

key_terms.definition: An instruction that tells the computer to do something. Always ends with a semicolon.
Code wrapped inside curly brackets { }. Used for class body and method body.
Values passed to a method, written inside round brackets.

tables.title: Where to Use Each Symbol
rows: Around class body and method body
After method names and inside print commands

tables.title: Semicolon: YES or NO?
rows: It is a class declaration (uses { instead)
It is a method declaration (uses { instead)

tables.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
rows: Missing semicolon at end of statement
Java thinks text is a variable name
Using ( ) instead of { } for code blocks
Using single quotes instead of double quotes

examples: CORRECT: System.out.println("Hello"); — statement ends with semicolon, text in double quotes, value in round brackets.
CORRECT: public class School { } — class declaration followed by curly brackets, no semicolon.
WRONG: public class School; — do not put semicolon after class declaration.
WRONG: System.out.println(Hello); — text must be in double quotes: "Hello".
EASY WAY TO REMEMBER: Semicolons are like full stops. Curly brackets are like boxes. Round brackets are like hands holding things. Double quotes are like speech marks.
